segments.text: Then the Israelites traveled to the plains of Moab and camped along the Jordan across from Jericho.
Now Balak son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to the Amorites,
and Moab was terrified because there were so many people. Indeed, Moab was filled with dread because of the Israelites.
The Moabites said to the elders of Midian,
This horde is going to lick up everything around us, as an ox licks up the grass of the field.
segments.speaker: Moabites

segments.text: So Balak son of Zippor, who was king of Moab at that time,
sent messengers to summon Balaam son of Beor, who was at Pethor, near the Euphrates River, in his native land. Balak said:
A people has come out of Egypt; they cover the face of the land and have settled next to me.
segments.speaker: Balak

segments.text: Now come and put a curse on these people, because they are too powerful for me. Perhaps then I will be able to defeat them and drive them out of the land. For I know that whoever you bless is blessed, and whoever you curse is cursed.
segments.speaker: Balak

segments.text: The elders of Moab and Midian left, taking with them the fee for divination. When they came to Balaam, they told him what Balak had said.
Balaam said to them,
Spend the night here, and I will report back to you with the answer the Lord gives me.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: So the Moabite officials stayed with him.
Who are these men with you?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Balaam said to God,
Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, sent me this message:
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: A people that has come out of Egypt covers the face of the land. Now come and put a curse on them for me. Perhaps then I will be able to fight them and drive them away.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Do not go with them. You must not put a curse on those people, because they are blessed.
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: The next morning Balaam got up and said to Balak's officials,
Go back to your own country, for the Lord has refused to let me go with you.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: So the Moabite officials returned to Balak and said,
Balaam refused to come with us.
segments.speaker: Moabite officials

segments.text: Then Balak sent other officials, more numerous and more distinguished than the first.
They came to Balaam and said:
This is what Balak son of Zippor says: Do not let anything keep you from coming to me,
segments.speaker: Balak's officials

segments.text: because I will reward you handsomely and do whatever you say. Come and put a curse on these people for me.
segments.speaker: Balak's officials

segments.text: Even if Balak gave me all the silver and gold in his palace, I could not do anything great or small to go beyond the command of the Lord my God.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Now spend the night here so that I can find out what else the Lord will tell me.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Since these men have come to summon you, go with them, but do only what I tell you.
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Balaam got up in the morning, saddled his donkey and went with the Moabite officials.
But God was very angry when he went, and the angel of the Lord stood in the road to oppose him. Balaam was riding on his donkey, and his two servants were with him.
When the donkey saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with a drawn sword in his hand, it turned off the road into a field. Balaam beat it to get it back on the road.
Then the angel of the Lord stood in a narrow path through the vineyards, with walls on both sides.
When the donkey saw the angel of the Lord, it pressed close to the wall, crushing Balaam's foot against it. So he beat the donkey again.
Then the angel of the Lord moved on ahead and stood in a narrow place where there was no room to turn, either to the right or to the left.
When the donkey saw the angel of the Lord, it lay down under Balaam, and he was angry and beat it with his staff.
What have I done to you to make you beat me these three times?
segments.speaker: Donkey

segments.text: You have made a fool of me! If only I had a sword in my hand, I would kill you right now.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Am I not your own donkey, which you have always ridden, to this day? Have I been in the habit of doing this to you?
segments.speaker: Donkey

segments.text: No
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Then the Lord opened Balaam's eyes, and he saw the angel of the Lord standing in the road with his sword drawn. So he bowed low and fell facedown.

Why have you beaten your donkey these three times? I have come here to oppose you because your path is a reckless one before me. The donkey saw me and turned away from me these three times. If it had not turned away, I would certainly have killed you by now, but I would have spared it.
segments.speaker: Angel of the Lord

segments.text: I have sinned. I did not realize you were standing in the road to oppose me. Now if you are displeased, I will go back.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Go with the men, but speak only what I tell you.
segments.speaker: Angel of the Lord

segments.text: So Balaam went with Balak's officials.
When Balak heard that Balaam was coming, he went out to meet him at the Moabite town on the Arnon border, at the edge of his territory.
Balak said to Balaam,
Did I not send you an urgent summons? Why didn't you come to me? Am I really not able to reward you?
segments.speaker: Balak

segments.text: Balaam replied,
Well, I have come to you now. But I can't say whatever I please. I must speak only what God puts in my mouth.
segments.speaker: Balaam

segments.text: Then Balaam went with Balak to Kiriath Huzoth.
Balak sacrificed cattle and sheep, and gave some to Balaam and the officials who were with him.
The next morning Balak took Balaam up to Bamoth Baal, and from there he could see the outskirts of the Israelite camp.
